title: On the kernel of the non abelian Fourier transform
speaker: Ngo Bao Chau
abstract: Tate reformulated the theory of the Riemann zeta function and its functional equation as the Mellin shadow of the Fourier transform on a certain space of function on the adeles. Conjecturally, Langlands' general automorphic L-functions and their functional equation can be interpreted in the same way following a framework due to Braverman and Kazhdan with the case of standard L-function associated with automorphic representations of GL_n and the standard representation of the dual GL_n being well known and due to Godement and Jacquet.

proof_stages.label: Langlands L-functions and functional-equation target
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up reductive groups over a global field, a dual-group representation rho, automorphic representations with unramified Satake parameters, the associated incomplete and completed L-functions, and the expected analytic continuation and functional equation.

proof_stages.label: Godement-Jacquet Fourier model
proof_stages.summary: For the standard representation of GL_n, the speaker recalls the embedding into the vector space of matrices, Schwartz spaces with basic functions, ordinary Fourier transform, Poisson summation, and Mellin transforms producing local L-factors and the global functional equation.

proof_stages.label: Braverman-Kazhdan nonabelian Fourier programme
proof_stages.summary: The Godement-Jacquet ingredients are abstracted to a general pair (G,rho): a monoid compactification M_rho, local Schwartz spaces and basic vectors, a rho-Fourier transform, and a Poisson summation formula, with the speaker indicating which parts are settled and which remain conjectural.

proof_stages.label: Monoids and IC basic functions
proof_stages.summary: Under favorable hypotheses, the required monoids are constructed by combinatorial and Vinberg-type methods, and the unramified basic function is identified geometrically as the trace of the IC sheaf on the relevant arc/task space, yielding the desired local factor.

proof_stages.label: Kernel desiderata and torus-to-Weyl descent
proof_stages.summary: The Fourier transform is reformulated as convolution with a distributional kernel J_rho characterized by support, algebraicity, stabilization of basic functions, and gamma-factor eigenvalues; for tori the weight data give an explicit Bessel-type kernel, and for split reductive groups the construction is pushed through the Weyl/Chevalley quotient to regular semisimple elements.

proof_stages.label: Finite-field sign correction
proof_stages.summary: In the finite-field analogue, naive Weyl descent of the toric hypergeometric/Kloosterman sheaf is shown to require a sign twist determined by the Weyl action on the weight multiset, leading to the correct sheaf/function and a categorical proof via vanishing and Mellin-transform properties.

proof_stages.label: Local-field correction and GL_n pseudo-differential operator
proof_stages.summary: Because the finite-field sign correction does not directly make sense over local fields, Ngô's GL_2 Fourier-in-trace correction is generalized to GL_n as a Fourier-multiplier or pseudo-differential operator involving explicit polynomials D_N, and its compatibility with descent is checked through constant-term and block-matrix computations.

proof_stages.label: Expected scope and open analytic issues
proof_stages.summary: The concluding discussion emphasizes the hope that the same operator yields kernels for arbitrary rho and broader families of L-functions, while convergence, action on all relevant representations, and the still-missing Schwartz-space theory remain open.

examples.name: Symplectic similitude monoid sketch
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker sketches how the monoid for symplectic similitudes can be recovered from embeddings through fundamental representations and closures, illustrating the general Vinberg/monoid construction without driving the argument.
examples.engagement_reason: he sketches how to construct the symplectic similitude monoid by writing the similitude condition, embedding via fundamental representations/matrix coefficients, adding scalars, and taking a closure

examples.name: Split GL1 torus kernel as Bessel/Kloosterman function
examples.note: For the split torus with the standard positive weight data, the kernel becomes a fiber integral giving a Bessel function, or Kloosterman sums over finite fields, visibly motivating the noncommutative kernel construction.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs the split GL1 torus case explicitly, identifying the compactification, describing the Schwartz germs, and writing the kernel as a fiber integral/Bessel function, with the finite-field Kloosterman analogue

passes.reason: The split GL1 torus is the rank-one abelian base case, and its Bessel/Kloosterman kernel is presented as the correct model.

examples.name: GL2 symmetric-square check
examples.note: The symmetric-square case for GL2 is cited as a known or formally checkable instance where the proposed local correction gives the expected kernel, serving as evidence rather than as a proof step.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the correction formula to GL2 using trace and determinant coordinates, Fourier transforms in the trace variable, and says the formal check gives the right kernel in the symmetric-square case
